items.prompt_md: In the one-pass stack solution for matching brackets, what is the first thing you do the moment you see a **closing** bracket?
options: Push it onto the stack for later
Pop the stack and check it equals the matching opener (and that the stack isn't empty)
Scan back through the whole string to find its opener
Increment a counter and keep going
items.answer: Pop the stack and check it equals the matching opener (and that the stack isn't empty)
items.explanation_md: The top of the stack is always the most recently opened, still-unmatched bracket — the only thing a closer can legally match. Popping and comparing is O(1); an empty stack at this point means there's nothing to close, so it's an immediate failure.

items.prompt_md: You're scanning a sequence where each token either opens or closes a nested structure, and it must nest correctly. Which structure fits?
options: A queue, so the first opener closes first
A stack, so the most recently opened item closes first
A hash set to track which brackets have appeared
Two pointers moving inward from both ends
items.answer: A stack, so the most recently opened item closes first
items.explanation_md: "Most recently opened, first closed" is LIFO by definition. A queue would enforce FIFO order instead, which is the wrong order for nested matching; a hash set or two pointers throw away the order information entirely.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the one-pass stack solution for validating nested brackets?
options: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
items.explanation_md: Each character is pushed or popped at most once, so it's a single O(n) pass; the stack can hold up to n items (e.g. a string of all openers), so space is O(n) in the worst case.
